welcome_message: Welcome to BoxLings! We'll teach you BoxLang AND test-driven development. For exercises with tests, read the test file first to understand what your code should do!
final_message: Congratulations! You've mastered BoxLang fundamentals. Keep practicing and building amazing things with BoxLang!

exercises.name: intro1
exercises.hint: This exercise is just to verify your setup. Enter 'n' to move on to the next exercise.

exercises.name: intro2
exercises.hint: The compiler is telling you that the function name is wrong. Read the error message carefully - or use the IDE to give you suggestions!

exercises.name: variables1
exercises.hint: In BoxLang, variables inside functions are automatically local-scoped (unlike CFML). Using 'var' is optional but recommended for clarity.

exercises.name: variables2
exercises.hint: BoxLang automatically assigns null to variables without a value. Try assigning different values (42, 'hello', [1,2,3]) to see type inference in action!

exercises.name: variables3
exercises.hint: Read the test file first! It tells you exactly what values to use. The test expects x=10, y=20, and sum=30.

exercises.name: variables4
exercises.hint: In BoxLang, variables are dynamically typed - you can reassign them to different types!

exercises.name: variables5
exercises.hint: Constants in BoxLang... well, BoxLang doesn't have true constants like other languages. Variables can always be reassigned!

exercises.name: functions1
exercises.hint: This main function is calling a function that doesn't exist yet. You need to create a function called call_me() that prints a message. Functions in BoxLang are declared using the 'function' keyword.

exercises.name: functions2
exercises.hint: The greet() function is trying to use a variable called 'name', but it doesn't have access to it. You need to add 'name' as a parameter to the function signature.

exercises.name: functions3
exercises.hint: Your function calculates the sum but doesn't return it! Functions need the 'return' keyword to send a value back to the caller.

exercises.name: functions4
exercises.hint: In BoxLang, you can provide default values for parameters using the = syntax in the function signature. This way, if the argument isn't provided, the default is used.

exercises.name: functions5
exercises.hint: BoxLang has two types of function expressions: Closures (=>) and Lambdas (->). Closures use the FAT ARROW (=>) and can access variables from their surrounding scope. Lambdas use the skinny arrow (->) and are pure functions.

exercises.name: functions6
exercises.hint: When using array methods like map(), filter(), reduce(), you typically use LAMBDAS (->), not closures (=>). Lambdas are pure functions that just transform data without side effects.

exercises.name: conditionals1
exercises.hint: Your function only returns a value when a > b. What happens when b is bigger or they're equal? You need an else clause!

exercises.name: conditionals2
exercises.hint: The ternary operator is BoxLang's shorthand for if/else. Syntax: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse

exercises.name: conditionals3
exercises.hint: Your switch handles weekdays but not weekends! Add cases for Saturday and Sunday.

exercises.name: conditionals4
exercises.hint: Think carefully about AND vs OR. For voting: BOTH conditions must be true. For teenagers: age must be BETWEEN 13 AND 19.

exercises.name: datatypes1
exercises.hint: In BoxLang, the + operator is for numbers. Use & (ampersand) to concatenate strings!

exercises.name: datatypes2
exercises.hint: Use int() to convert a decimal to an integer, or use the backslash operator for integer division.

exercises.name: datatypes3
exercises.hint: The string 'true' is not the same as the boolean true. Remove the quotes!

exercises.name: datatypes4
exercises.hint: Use dateAdd() to add time to a date. Format: dateAdd('d', amount, date)

exercises.name: datatypes5
exercises.hint: Arrays use square brackets [ ], not curly braces { }!

exercises.name: datatypes6
exercises.hint: Structs use curly braces { }, not square brackets [ ]! And use : not = for key-value pairs.

exercises.name: datatypes7
exercises.hint: Ordered structs use square brackets [ ] with key:value syntax to preserve order!

exercises.name: arrays1
exercises.hint: Arrays in BoxLang start at index 1, not 0! This is different from most languages.

exercises.name: arrays2
exercises.hint: Use the append() member function to add items to an array.

exercises.name: structs1
exercises.hint: Check the struct definition - what keys does it actually have? Use the correct property name.

exercises.name: structs2
exercises.hint: BoxLang struct keys are case-insensitive! NAME, name, NaMe all refer to the same key.

exercises.name: structs3
exercises.hint: Use count() to get the number of keys, and keyExists() to check if a key exists.

exercises.name: structs4
exercises.hint: Nested structs are accessed with dot notation: outer.inner.property

exercises.name: structs5
exercises.hint: Ordered structs use square brackets [ ] with key:value syntax, not curly braces { }.

exercises.name: strings1
exercises.hint: BoxLang uses ## for expression interpolation, not $ like some other languages.

exercises.name: strings2
exercises.hint: In BoxLang, just continue your string on the next line - no special syntax needed!

exercises.name: strings3
exercises.hint: Use ucase() for uppercase and trim() to remove whitespace.

exercises.name: strings4
exercises.hint: The & operator concatenates strings. The + operator is for numbers!

exercises.name: strings5
exercises.hint: Use listToArray( string, delimiter ) to convert a delimited string to an array.

exercises.name: strings6
exercises.hint: Escape quotes by doubling them: "" or use single quotes on the outside.

exercises.name: imports1
exercises.hint: Use the import statement at the top of your class: import ClassName

exercises.name: imports2
exercises.hint: Java classes need the java: prefix. Example: import java:java.util.ArrayList

exercises.name: imports3
exercises.hint: When using an alias, create instances with the alias name: new AliasName()

exercises.name: imports4
exercises.hint: After importing, create an instance with: new HashMap()

exercises.name: structs_advanced1
exercises.hint: Use structAppend( target, source, true ) to merge with overwrite.

exercises.name: structs_advanced2
exercises.hint: Use duplicate() to create a deep copy.

exercises.name: structs_advanced3
exercises.hint: Use scores.each( ( key, value ) => { doubled[ key ] = value * 2 } )

exercises.name: structs_advanced4
exercises.hint: Use structKeyArray() then arraySort( keys, "text" ).

exercises.name: nulls1
exercises.hint: Use null coalescing: name ?: "guest"

exercises.name: nulls2
exercises.hint: Check for null before accessing user.profile.email.

exercises.name: nulls3
exercises.hint: Use data.keyExists( "token" ) to decide a default.

exercises.name: nulls4
exercises.hint: Default to "friend" when name is null.

exercises.name: errors1
exercises.hint: Set handled = true in the catch block.

exercises.name: errors2
exercises.hint: Capture the custom exception type from e.type.

exercises.name: errors3
exercises.hint: Finally always runs - set cleaned = true there.

exercises.name: errors4
exercises.hint: Throw with message "Amount cannot be negative".

exercises.name: errors5
exercises.hint: Use e.detail to capture the detail string.

exercises.name: errors6
exercises.hint: Throw an error when b == 0 to hit the catch.

exercises.name: interfaces1
exercises.hint: Implement run() and set variables.ran = true.

exercises.name: interfaces2
exercises.hint: Return -1, 0, or 1 based on comparing value with other.value.

exercises.name: interfaces3
exercises.hint: Supplier#get should return "Hello BoxLang".

exercises.name: interfaces4
exercises.hint: AutoCloseable requires a close() method.

exercises.name: testing1
exercises.hint: Write tests that validate add( a, b ).

exercises.name: testing2
exercises.hint: Write tests for reverseString().

exercises.name: testing3
exercises.hint: Write tests for buildUser( name, age ).

exercises.name: testing4
exercises.hint: Write tests for sumArray().

exercises.name: testing5
exercises.hint: Write tests for isEven().

exercises.name: functional1
exercises.hint: Use map( ( n ) -> n * 2 ).

exercises.name: functional2
exercises.hint: Filter even numbers with n % 2 == 0.

exercises.name: functional3
exercises.hint: Use reduce( ( acc, n ) -> acc + n, 0 ).

exercises.name: functional4
exercises.hint: Use each() with a closure to accumulate total.

exercises.name: functional5
exercises.hint: Map words to their lengths with word.len().

exercises.name: functional6
exercises.hint: Filter evens, then map to n * 2.

exercises.name: functional7
exercises.hint: Use arrayFind( numbers, 3 ).

exercises.name: functional8
exercises.hint: Use reduce() to build a phrase, then trim().

exercises.name: async1
exercises.hint: asyncRun() accepts a closure and returns a BoxFuture. Call .get() to retrieve the result synchronously.

exercises.name: async2
exercises.hint: asyncRun( () => number * 2 ) creates the future. .get() waits for and returns the computed value.

exercises.name: async3
exercises.hint: asyncAll() takes an array of closures/futures and executes them in parallel. The BIF name is asyncAll().

exercises.name: async4
exercises.hint: asyncAny() races all given futures and returns the first one to complete. Use asyncAny( futures ).get().

exercises.name: async5
exercises.hint: BoxFuture supports .then( callback ) for chaining. Each .then() returns a new future with the transformed value.

exercises.name: async6
exercises.hint: .exceptionally( err => fallback ) catches errors from the future. Return -1 as the fallback value.

exercises.name: components1
exercises.hint: The bx:http component makes HTTP requests. Set url="https://httpbin.org/get" and method="get".

exercises.name: components2
exercises.hint: Use queryExecute() with dbtype="query" to query an in-memory query object. The SQL filter should be: age > 25.

exercises.name: components3
exercises.hint: bx:loop syntax: bx:loop array="#fruits#" item="fruit" { ... }. Use the item variable name to access each element.

exercises.name: casting1
exercises.hint: The castAs operator: value castAs type. Use int, double, and boolean as the type names.

exercises.name: casting2
exercises.hint: Use castAs String and castAs String[] (note: String[] for array casting).

exercises.name: casting3
exercises.hint: javaCast( type, value ) — types are lowercase Java primitive names: "int", "double", "long", "string".

exercises.name: casting4
exercises.hint: instanceOf checks the Java type. Strings are "java.lang.String", numbers are "java.lang.Number", booleans are "java.lang.Boolean".

exercises.name: casting5
exercises.hint: val() converts a string to a number. toString() converts a value to string. int() truncates a decimal to integer.

exercises.name: quiz1
exercises.hint: 1) Use #name# in the string literal. 2) ?: is null-coalescing. 3) The closure captures 'factor' from the outer scope. 4) ?. is safe navigation.

exercises.name: quiz2
exercises.hint: 1) n % 2 != 0 filters odds. 2) map() transforms each element — use .map(). 3) acc + n sums. 4) config.server.host accesses nested key.

exercises.name: quiz3
exercises.hint: Properties: name (string) and score (numeric). Fix variables.score +=. Grade: ternary chain. castAs int for the Java cast.

exercises.name: classes1
exercises.hint: property name="name" type="string" declares a property. BoxLang auto-generates getName()/setName() for you.

exercises.name: classes2
exercises.hint: For fluent chaining, each setter must return 'this'. The 'this' keyword refers to the current object instance.

exercises.name: classes3
exercises.hint: classes3Circle.bx extends classes3. Use super.init() to call the parent constructor. Override area() with pi() * radius^2.

exercises.name: classes4
exercises.hint: Declare static { count: 0 }. Increment with static.count += 1 in init(). Access with classes4::getCount().

exercises.name: classes5
exercises.hint: The pseudo-constructor runs before init(). Use now() to set variables.createdAt in the space between the last property and the first function.

exercises.name: classes6
exercises.hint: Annotations go on their own line before the class/function/property: @singleton before class, @required before property, @cached before function.

exercises.name: classes7
exercises.hint: this.$bx.meta contains the class metadata. Use .name for the class name and .properties for the array of defined properties.

exercises.name: classes8
exercises.hint: onMissingMethod( missingMethodName, missingMethodArgs ) is called automatically. Use arguments.missingMethodName in the return string.

exercises.name: bifs1
exercises.hint: Member functions mirror the BIF names: trim(), uCase(), contains(), replace(), len(). Call them directly on the string variable.

exercises.name: bifs2
exercises.hint: Array member functions: sort(), find(), unique(), sum(). hasNine should store the INDEX where 9 was found (> 0 means found).

exercises.name: bifs3
exercises.hint: Struct member functions: keyExists(), keyArray(), valueArray(), count(). All are called directly on the struct variable.

exercises.name: bifs4
exercises.hint: now() returns the current date. year( date ) extracts the year. dateFormat() formats a date. dateDiff( "d", d1, d2 ) computes day difference.

exercises.name: bifs5
exercises.hint: Math BIFs: abs(), int() for truncation, round(), min(), max(). Power is: 2^10 using the ^ operator or using the power() BIF.

exercises.name: bifs6
exercises.hint: getFunctionList() returns a struct. Use .count() for size, .keyExists( name ) to check for a specific BIF.

exercises.name: templating1
exercises.hint: In bxm templates, use #variableName# for interpolation inside bx:output. Replace both ??? with the variable names: title and version.

exercises.name: templating2
exercises.hint: bx:loop array="#fruits#" item="fruit" { ... }. Inside the loop, use #fruit# to output each item.

exercises.name: templating3
exercises.hint: bx:if uses comparison operators: >= for greater-than-or-equal. Fix both ??? — the first condition needs >= 90, the second >= 70.

exercises.name: templating4
exercises.hint: Inside bx:loop, access item properties with item.price and item.name. The total accumulates with each iteration. Pass total to numberFormat().

exercises.name: cli_apps1
exercises.hint: args is an array. Access the first element with args[1]. The ternary args.len() > 0 ? args[1] : "World" provides the default.

exercises.name: cli_apps2
exercises.hint: CLIGetArgs() returns { positionals: [], options: {} }. Access positionals with .positionals and options with .options.

exercises.name: cli_apps3
exercises.hint: args[1] is num1, args[2] is the operation, args[3] is num2. Fix the index numbers in the ??? placeholders.

exercises.name: cli_apps4
exercises.hint: getSystemSetting( "KEY", "default" ) reads from environment variables or JVM system properties. Fill in the env var names: APP_NAME, APP_PORT, APP_DEBUG.

exercises.name: java_interop1
exercises.hint: The full class path is java.lang.StringBuilder. After building, call .toString() to convert the Java StringBuilder to a BoxLang string.

exercises.name: java_interop2
exercises.hint: import java:java.util.UUID imports the Java UUID class. Call UUID.randomUUID().toString() to get a UUID string.

exercises.name: java_interop3
exercises.hint: new java:java.util.ArrayList() creates a Java ArrayList. Java uses 0-based indexing: list.get(0) returns the first item.

exercises.name: java_interop4
exercises.hint: javaCast( "double", value ) casts to a Java double. For abs(-99), cast -99 as a long. JMath.PI is a field accessed with .PI.

exercises.name: java_interop5
exercises.hint: Catch Java exceptions using catch( "full.class.Name" e ). Set result to "caught" and store e.message in errorType.

exercises.name: java_interop6
exercises.hint: map.put( key, value ) adds entries. The third ??? should be the key "runtime" with value "JVM".
